itineraries.title: Ireland Food Trail: Farmers Markets and Artisan Distilleries
itineraries.subtitle: From Dublin to the Atlantic coast, a 10-day journey through the farmers markets, craft distilleries and producers who define Irish gastronomy.
itineraries.notes: Temple Bar Market runs on Saturdays only – plan Day 1 accordingly. Kenmare Farmers Market is held on Wednesdays. Westport Country Market is on Thursdays. Westport Food & Craft Market is on Saturdays. Book distillery visits in advance during high season.

map_keywords: Temple Bar Food Market Meeting House Square Dublin
Guinness Storehouse St James Gate Dublin

visits.text: Kick off your Dublin stay at Temple Bar Food Market on Meeting House Square. Founded in 1997 by a collective of Irish farmers, this Saturday market runs from 10am to 4:30pm beneath a retractable canopy that shields you from the rain. You'll meet producers who've held their stalls since day one: sourdough bakers, farmhouse cheesemakers, artisan butchers and cold-pressed juice vendors. Arrive early—the sourdough sells out fast.

Next, step inside the Guinness Storehouse at St James's Gate. The 1904 fermentation building was reimagined as a museum in 2000, its seven storeys shaped like a giant pint glass. On the ground floor, beneath a glass panel, you'll spot the 9,000-year lease signed by Arthur Guinness in 1759—for £45 a year. Ride the lift to the Gravity Bar at the top: your complimentary pint comes with a 360° panorama across Dublin's rooftops. Allow 2 to 3 hours and book online to skip the queues.

Finish with a stroll down Moore Street, Dublin's oldest open-air market, trading since the 18th century. The street carries serious history—this is where the leaders of the 1916 Easter Rising, including Patrick Pearse, surrendered to British forces. Today, fruit-and-veg stalls jostle with Chinese grocers, African spice vendors and Indian sweet shops—a snapshot of modern, cosmopolitan Dublin. Don't miss the handmade dumplings and Bolivian plates tucked away in the basement mall.

activities.text: Join an Irish cookery class to master the basics of soda bread, colcannon and boxty—dishes that look straightforward but require a deft hand. Several city-centre workshops offer 2- to 3-hour sessions in small groups, with all kit and ingredients provided. Book ahead, especially at weekends.

In late afternoon, let a food-tour guide lead you through Temple Bar. You'll sample oysters, farmhouse cheeses and charcuterie while learning Dublin's culinary backstory, weaving between cobbled lanes and centuries-old pubs. Come hungry and wear comfortable shoes.

visits.text: On arrival in Kilkenny, head straight for Parliament Street and the Smithwick's Experience. Franciscan monks brewed beer here from the 13th century at St Francis Abbey. In 1710 a man named John Smithwick founded his own brewery on the site—under the Penal Laws, he wasn't even allowed to display his name on the façade. The guided tour lasts roughly an hour: you'll mill malt, stir the mash and inhale the hops before finishing with a pint of red ale. If you're keen, opt for the Masters of Ale package to compare three different ales side by side.

Wander through Kilkenny's farmers market, held in the medieval heart of town a stone's throw from the castle. You'll meet county producers selling cheeses, artisan breads, local honey and homemade jams. The atmosphere is friendly, and the stallholders love sharing the stories behind their goods.

Drive 15 minutes south to Stoneyford to visit Knockdrinna, an artisan cheese dairy founded in 2004 by Helen Finnegan. She crafts goat's-, sheep's- and cow's-milk cheeses, including Kilree—a washed-rind goat's cheese that won Supreme Champion at the British Cheese Awards. The farm runs a charming café where you can taste cheese boards with house-made chutneys, plus a small petting farm with ducks, hens and miniature ponies. Full-day cheesemaking courses are available—well worth booking if the subject grips you.

activities.text: Treat yourself to an Irish cheese-tasting workshop in Kilkenny town. Several local producers run guided sessions where you'll learn to tell an aged cheddar from a creamy blue, a fresh chèvre from a hard sheep's-milk cheese. Reckon on an hour, with bread-jam-cheese pairings that will reconcile you with Ireland's terroir.

Visit an organic farm on the outskirts of Kilkenny. The county teems with small holdings that practise sustainable agriculture: free-range livestock, organic market gardens, wildlife hedgerows for biodiversity. Some farms welcome visitors for a guided walk through the fields, often with fresh-produce tastings to finish.

visits.text: Cork's English Market has been the city's beating heart since 1788—one of the world's oldest municipal covered markets. The name comes from the Protestant corporation that established it, to distinguish it from the neighbouring 'Irish market'. Beneath red-brick arches and stained-glass skylights you'll find specialities you won't see anywhere else: drisheen (local black pudding), Cork spiced beef, buttered eggs and tripe. Chef Rick Stein called it the finest covered market in the British Isles. Head upstairs to the Farmgate Café, which cooks straight from the stalls below. Queen Elizabeth II made a point of visiting this market during her historic 2011 trip.

If time allows, make the 40-minute detour to Shanagarry to explore Ballymaloe Cookery School, one of Ireland's most celebrated culinary schools, set on a 100-acre estate with organic vegetable gardens, orchards and glasshouses. Half-day classes are open to enthusiasts—book well in advance.

Continue to Midleton (25 km east of Cork) for the distillery that produces Jameson. The 15-acre site started life as a woollen mill before being converted into a military barracks, then a distillery in 1825 by the Murphy brothers. You'll see the world's largest copper pot still—so enormous they had to assemble it on-site and build the warehouse around it. The guided tour takes you through oak-cask maturation cellars and ends with a tasting commentary. Go for the premium package if you'd like to sample rare expressions such as Redbreast or Midleton Very Rare.

activities.text: Treat yourself to an Irish baking class to perfect the scone, porter cake (stout cake) and proper Irish apple tart. Two-hour workshops run in Cork city centre and the surrounding area, often led by chefs trained at Ballymaloe.

Finish the day with an artisan cider tasting. County Cork is home to several producers who work local apples using traditional methods—slow fermentation, no additives. You'll discover dry, off-dry and sparkling ciders that bear no resemblance to industrial versions.

visits.text: Kenmare Farmers Market runs every Wednesday on the town's central square. Time your visit accordingly: the stalls sell local farmhouse cheeses, sourdough breads, smoked fish and organic vegetables grown in peninsula gardens. The atmosphere is laid-back, and producers love talking through their methods.

Around Kenmare and Sneem, several artisan cheese dairies are worth a detour. They produce farmhouse cheeses from Jersey cow's milk in small batches—plain, aged, cumin or garlic-flavoured—sold mainly at local markets and to area restaurants. Check with Kenmare's tourist office to find out which dairies welcome visitors on the day you're passing through.

Don't leave Kenmare without visiting Lorge chocolatier, located in Bonane 5 km along the Glengarriff road. Benoît Lorge, a French pâtissier from Lorraine, trained across the world—Corsica, southern France, Scotland—before settling in Kerry. He handcrafts rum-caramel bonbons, whiskey truffles and paprika pralines that supply Ireland's finest hotels. You assemble your own box by choosing each chocolate from the display counters—a pleasure in itself. Ninety-minute chocolate workshops are available for families and groups, and kids love them.

activities.text: Treat yourself to an oyster tasting on Kenmare Bay. The bay's cold, nutrient-rich waters produce wild oysters with firm, briny flesh. Several operators offer tastings with white wine or stout pairings, right on the shoreline. If you're there late in the day, the light across the bay is superb.

Make the most of Kerry's wild landscape with a wild-berry and edible-herb foraging session led by a local guide. Blackberries, sloes, wild garlic and mushrooms are easily found in the peninsula's hedgerows and woodlands, depending on the season. It's an original way to understand local cuisine through its humblest ingredients.

map_keywords: Murphy's Ice Cream Strand Street Dingle

visits.text: Dingle Distillery, founded in 2012 in a former sawmill on Milltown Road, is one of the first of Ireland's new wave of micro-distilleries. It uses three bespoke copper pot stills made in Scotland, with a boil ball that encourages reflux and gives the spirit remarkable smoothness. The gin is infused with local botanicals: rowan berries, fuchsia, bog myrtle, hawthorn and heather—a pure distillation of the peninsula. The guided tour runs roughly 75 minutes, with tastings of whiskey, gin and vodka. Book online, especially in summer.

Walk down to Dingle harbour to discover the fish market. Boats come in each morning with crab, lobster, mackerel and scallops caught in the bay. The atmosphere is raw and authentic—a long way from polished display cases. If you're there on a Friday, the Dingle Farmers Market on the square also offers local cheeses, artisan bread and ready meals.

Stop at Murphy's Ice Cream on Strand Street, a local institution that makes its ice cream from 100% Irish ingredients: Kerry milk, Dingle sea salt, brown bread, salted-butter caramel. Try the Dingle Sea Salt or Brown Bread flavour—tastes you won't find anywhere else. The shop is tiny and often has a queue in summer, but it's worth the wait.

activities.text: Join an Irish fish-and-chips workshop. Local operators in Dingle offer lessons in battering fish in beer batter, frying perfect chips and mastering mushy peas (mashed peas). You'll leave with simple recipes and cooking tips.

If the weather plays along, board a fishing boat departing Dingle harbour. You'll watch traditional fishing techniques, with any luck spot dolphins in the bay, and might even return with fresh mackerel you can grill that evening. Bring warm, waterproof clothing—even in summer, the wind blows hard offshore.

map_keywords: Burren Gold Cheese Aillwee Ballyvaughan Clare

visits.text: Stop in Lisdoonvarna, 10 minutes from Doolin, to visit the Burren Smokehouse. This family-run artisan smokery, founded in 1989 by Birgitta and Peter Curtin, produces organic smoked salmon that has won Great Taste Awards. You'll smell the oak smoke as soon as you cross the threshold. The free visitor centre explains hot- and cold-smoking processes through videos in six languages and mosaics retelling the legend of the Salmon of Knowledge. The Swedish royal family visited in person in 2019 to taste their salmon. Just next door, don't miss the 'Taste the Atlantic – Salmon Experience', an interactive trail launched the same year.

Continue to Ballyvaughan, 20 minutes from Lisdoonvarna, to visit Burren Gold Cheese dairy, housed in the Farm Shop at the Aillwee site. Since 1985 they've been making farmhouse gouda from the milk of a 65-strong herd of Holstein Friesians that graze on the wild grasses and flowers of the Burren, 5 km away. The cheesemaker milks the cows just once a day—which yields richer, creamier milk. You'll taste six variants: plain, oak-smoked (one of Ireland's last natural smokehouses), cumin, garlic-nettle, black pepper and piri-piri. Wheels aged 1 or 2 years develop surprising caramel and hazelnut notes. The cheese holds the GEOfood label of the Burren UNESCO Global Geopark—from field to plate, everything is produced within a few kilometres.

If you're passing on a Friday or Saturday, detour via Ennis farmers market, 30 minutes south of Doolin. You'll meet Clare producers selling cheeses, smoked meats, breads and honey in the typical atmosphere of an Irish market town, far quieter than Dublin or Cork markets.

activities.text: Join an edible-seaweed foraging session on the Burren coast. A guide will take you at low tide to identify dulse (reddish, nutty taste), sea lettuce and carrageen used as a natural gelling agent. You'll learn how to cook them—in salads, as seasoning or in seaweed butter. Bring rubber boots.

Finish the evening with a seafood-chowder crawl through Doolin's pubs. This tiny village boasts three historic pubs renowned for traditional music and chowder—a thick, creamy seafood soup. Compare recipes from pub to pub: some add salmon, others crab, all serve a chunk of soda bread for mopping. The musical atmosphere in the evening is an incomparable bonus.

visits.text: Galway is the gastronomic capital of the west of Ireland. Start at Saturday's market on Church Lane, right in the city centre: Clarinbridge oysters, Connemara cheeses, artisan breads, crêpes and coffee from local roasters. Galway's bohemian spirit runs through every stall. If you're after a memorable meal, several city-centre restaurants build their menus around short supply chains with market producers.

Swing by Galway Atlantaquaria in Salthill, Ireland's largest aquarium. You'll see Atlantic-coast species—rays, conger eels, lobsters, jellyfish—and understand the marine ecosystem that feeds all the region's seafood cooking. Kids love the touch pool where they can handle starfish and sea urchins.

After the market, step into Sheridans Cheesemongers on Churchyard Street, right beside St Nicholas's Church. It all began in 1995 with a simple cheese stall at Saturday's market—today it's Ireland's most celebrated cheesemonger. The shop, long and narrow, stacks floor-to-ceiling wheels of Dutch gouda, comté, aged Irish cheddar and creamy blues. Ask the cheesemongers for advice: they'll let you sample half a dozen Irish farmhouse cheeses with anecdotes about each producer. Then climb upstairs to the wine bar for a cheese-and-charcuterie board with a glass of natural wine—the perfect spot for a light lunch facing the medieval church.

activities.text: If you're in Galway in late September, you'll land right in the middle of the International Oyster & Seafood Festival, the world's oldest oyster festival (founded in 1954). On the programme: oyster-opening competitions, tastings, concerts and street parades. Even outside festival season, Galway remains Ireland's best city for eating oysters—ask for Clarinbridge natives.

practical_info.booking: Festival late September – check exact dates

activities.text: Treat yourself to an oyster-and-champagne tasting class. Several operators in Galway offer lessons in opening oysters yourself, recognising varieties (flat, rock, wild) and pairing them with bubbles or a glass of stout. Reckon on roughly 90 minutes for the full session.

visits.text: Clifden is the 'capital of Connemara', a small, colourful town wedged between mountain and ocean. Start with a stroll along Main Street and Market Street—the windows of fishmongers and delicatessens overflow with smoked salmon, fresh crab and artisan pâtés. Several seafood restaurants display the day's catch on a chalkboard at the door.

If you're there on a Saturday, Clifden's artisan market gathers local producers and craftspeople: goat's cheeses, sourdough breads, wild-berry jams, Connemara honey. It's small but authentic—a far cry from tourist markets.

Fifteen minutes south of Clifden, on the Ballyconneely road, stop at Connemara Smokehouse at the end of Bunowen pier. It's the oldest smokery in Connemara, founded in 1979 by the Roberts family. The original smokehouse, christened 'Old Smoky', dates from 1946 and still runs on beech wood. You'll watch the master smoker fillet salmon by hand—he cuts 25 to 30 sides an hour, with a precision impossible to tell from a machine. Taste the cold-smoked wild salmon, smoked line-caught tuna (which chef Rick Stein serves at his Padstow restaurant in Cornwall) and gravlax cured in Irish whiskey. The view from the pier across the Twelve Bens and Grace O'Malley's castle is worth the trip alone. Guided tours in summer by reservation.

activities.text: Attend a salmon-smoking workshop in the Clifden or Connemara area. You'll learn the steps—sea-salt brining, air-drying, slow smoking over oak or beech wood—and leave with your own piece of smoked salmon. These workshops are usually run by small, passionate producers who take the time to explain each gesture.

Visit a Connemara lamb farm. Connemara lamb has European PGI status—the sheep graze freely on heather and bog-covered hills, giving the meat a unique herbal flavour. Some farms welcome visitors for a walk with the shepherd and his dogs, followed by a tasting of lamb dishes.

map_keywords: Westport Country Market James Street Westport

visits.text: Step into Cornrue artisan bakery, on the heights above Westport. Founded in 2017, it started in an oven borrowed from a local restaurant, baking through the night. Today the team starts each morning at 6:15am to produce organic-flour sourdough loaves, scones and brown bread. The bread sells fast—arrive before 10am for the full selection. The shop also stocks seasonal vegetables, homemade jams and natural wines.

Westport Country Market runs every Thursday from 8:30am to 1pm at the Pete Callaghan Centre near James Street. You'll meet Mayo producers selling artisan cheeses, honey, charcuterie and home-baked cakes. On Saturdays it's the Food & Craft Market on the Mall that takes over, from 9am to 5pm, with an even wider choice. Adapt your day to the right market.

Ten minutes from Westport, at the foot of Croagh Patrick, Mescan Brewery is a microbrewery founded in 2013 by two retrained vets: Cillian, Irish, and Bart, Belgian. Their water comes from a spring that rises beneath the holy mountain. They brew Belgian-inspired beers—saison, tripel, dubbel, and even a lambic aged 3 years in oak casks. The informal tour lasts roughly 90 minutes, with tastings of seven beers and snacks. Cillian and Bart love recounting how they left veterinary medicine to launch into beer. Book ahead.

activities.text: Embark on an oyster-farming trip on Clew Bay, the bay off Westport that harbours more than 300 islands and a long shellfish tradition. Local producers have cultivated oysters and mussels in these cold waters since the 1980s. Some offer tours of their oyster beds by boat, followed by a tasting on the quay with soda bread and a glass of stout. The view of Croagh Patrick from the bay is spectacular—hard to imagine a finer setting for eating oysters.

Before hitting the road back to Dublin, detour via Killary Harbour, Ireland's only fjord, 40 minutes south of Westport. Mussels have been farmed here in open water for decades, fed by Atlantic currents. Operators offer boat trips on the fjord, framed by 700-metre mountains, with tastings of smoked and fresh mussels straight on the water. If you prefer dry land, a roadside food truck serves fjord mussels in a relaxed atmosphere facing the landscape.

map_keywords: Chapter One Restaurant Parnell Square Dublin
Pearse Lyons Distillery James Street Dublin

visits.text: For your final day, treat yourself to lunch at one of Dublin's fine-dining restaurants. Several tables in the city centre, around Parnell Square, offer tasting menus built around Ireland's finest producers—the same ones you've met throughout the trip. If you book ahead, you can discover cuisine that elevates Irish terroir ingredients with finesse and creativity.

Stop by Fallon & Byrne, the food hall on Exchequer Street. This three-storey space—delicatessen on the ground floor, wine cellar in the basement, restaurant upstairs—gathers the best of Irish and European produce. Aged cheeses, artisan charcuterie, breads, pastries, olive oils... It's the perfect place to assemble a hamper of specialities to take home.

Finish with a visit to Pearse Lyons Distillery, housed in the former St James's Church, a stone's throw from the Guinness Storehouse on James's Street. The 12th-century church has been beautifully restored and now shelters copper stills amid stained glass. The tour tells the story of Dublin distilling—once home to 37 distilleries—and ends with a tasting in the nave. A spectacular setting, at the crossroads of the sacred and the spirited.

activities.text: Join an Irish-cocktails workshop at one of the city-centre cocktail bars. You'll learn to prepare a proper Irish Coffee (the secret: warm whiskey, unsweetened whipped cream), a Dingle-gin cocktail or an Irish whiskey Old Fashioned. Reckon on roughly 90 minutes for the session, relaxed atmosphere guaranteed.

Close your food trail with dinner at a restaurant that celebrates Irish produce. After 10 days touring the country's markets, farms and workshops, you'll recognise on the menu the cheeses, meats, fish and beers you discovered along the route. The best possible summary of this journey.

seo.h1_title: Ireland Food Trail: Farmers Markets and Artisan Distilleries
seo.intro_paragraph: From Dublin to the Atlantic coast, this 10-day circuit takes you to meet the farmers markets, craft distilleries and producers who are the pride of Irish gastronomy. Taste Burren smoked salmon, Galway oysters, Kilkenny cheeses and Midleton whiskey. Learn to brew, smoke, ferment and cook alongside passionate artisans.
seo.seo_hook: Farmers markets brimming with local produce, whiskey aged in oak casks, wild Atlantic oysters and cheeses matured in Kerry cellars—Irish cuisine is best discovered hands-on.
featured_list: Dublin: Temple Bar Food Market since 1997 and seven-storey Guinness Storehouse
Cork: English Market open since 1788, Ireland's oldest covered market
Galway: oyster capital and international festival founded in 1954
Westport: Mescan microbrewery at the foot of Croagh Patrick and Thursday farmers market

places_taglines.IE::dublin: Where Temple Bar market stalls meet Guinness heritage and Moore Street's cosmopolitan bustle
places_taglines.IE::kilkenny: Medieval brewhouse turned red-ale shrine and award-winning farmhouse cheeses
places_taglines.IE::cork: A Georgian market hall that's fed Cork since 1788, plus Ireland's whiskey heartland
places_taglines.IE::kenmare: French chocolatier meets Atlantic oysters in this elegant Kerry gateway
places_taglines.IE::dingle: Where wild botanicals flavour craft gin and peninsula waves shape the catch
places_taglines.IE::doolin: Smokehouse salmon kissed by Atlantic winds, limestone pastures and trad-music nights
places_taglines.IE::galway: Ireland's oyster throne, where Sheridans cheese reigns and September feasts the sea
places_taglines.IE::clifden: Connemara's colourful capital, framed by bog-grazed lamb and pier-smoked salmon
places_taglines.IE::westport: Sourdough mornings, Belgian-Irish brewing and Clew Bay oysters beneath Croagh Patrick

highlights: Guinness Storehouse: seven immersive floors with tasting at the panoramic Gravity Bar, 9,000-year lease signed in 1759
Cork's English Market: covered market since 1788, visited by Queen Elizabeth II in 2011
Dingle Distillery: micro-distillery founded in 2012, gin infused with wild peninsula botanicals
Burren Smokehouse: family smokery since 1989, visited by the Swedish royal couple in 2019

itineraries.title: Ireland Road Trip: Capitals, Coasts and Mountains
itineraries.subtitle: 15 days from Dublin to Belfast and back, through cliffs, pubs and monasteries lost in the mist
itineraries.notes: Donegal → Belfast route (D13→D14): 218 km, allow 3 hours' driving. Essential to book in advance for Kilmainham Gaol and the Book of Kells in Dublin. The Ring of Kerry should be driven anti-clockwise to avoid tour buses.

map_keywords: Book of Kells Old Library Dublin

visits.text: Start at Trinity College, founded in 1592 by Elizabeth I — it's Ireland's oldest university. You'll see the Book of Kells, an illuminated manuscript created around the year 800 by monks on the island of Iona in Scotland. The displayed pages change every twelve weeks, so each visit offers something different. The Long Room, a magnificent vaulted hall stretching 65 meters, ranks among the world's most beautiful libraries — George Lucas drew inspiration from it for the Jedi archives in Star Wars. Book your tickets online in advance — it's essential. Allow about 90 minutes for the full experience.

Head down to Temple Bar, Dublin's liveliest quarter. Cobbled lanes brim with colourful pubs where live music kicks off as early as 3pm. If you want atmosphere without the crush, visit midweek in early afternoon. Evenings pack out but the energy's infectious. Don't limit yourself to the main drag — the parallel side streets hide art galleries and vintage shops worth discovering.

Step inside the Chester Beatty, tucked within Dublin Castle's grounds. This free museum houses one of the world's finest manuscript collections, assembled by American mining magnate Alfred Chester Beatty. You'll find some of Christianity's oldest biblical papyri, Chinese jade books, Persian miniatures and even the Gospel of Mani. Lonely Planet ranked it among Europe's best museums. Stop by the Silk Road Café for lunch with Middle Eastern flavours.

Finish your day at Christ Church Cathedral, founded around 1030 by the Viking king Sitric Silkenbeard. Its medieval crypt, stretching 63 meters, is the largest in Ireland and Britain. You'll spot a mummified cat and rat trapped in an organ pipe — James Joyce mentioned them in Finnegans Wake. Head down to admire the 14th-century copy of Magna Carta. The cathedral holds a world record with its 19 swinging bells.

activities.text: Treat yourself to a late-afternoon stroll along the Liffey. From the Ha'penny Bridge — an elegant 1816 iron footbridge — you'll get perfect views of the illuminated quays. Continue to the Samuel Beckett Bridge, shaped like a harp lying on its side, Ireland's national symbol. It's the best time to feel Dublin's pulse among joggers, street musicians and quayside terraces.

In the evening, dive into a musical pub crawl. Dublin's the world capital of traditional music sessions — fiddle, bodhrán, tin whistle. Skip the overly touristy Temple Bar pubs and head instead for Stoneybatter or the pubs around Camden Street for a more local vibe. Guinness tastes different here, the Irish will tell you.

visits.text: First stop: Powerscourt Estate gardens in Enniskerry, ranked the world's third most beautiful gardens by National Geographic. The Palladian mansion was designed in 1731 by German architect Richard Cassels. From the Italian terraces, the view towards Sugar Loaf is spectacular. Don't miss the 1908 Japanese garden, the Pepperpot tower and the pet cemetery — the epitaphs are touching. Allow 2 to 3 hours to see everything properly. Powerscourt Waterfall, 6km away, is a separate site — at 121 meters, it's Ireland's highest waterfall.

Next, reach the monastic site of Glendalough, founded in the 6th century by Saint Kevin. This hermit monk first settled in a cave carved into the cliff above the upper lake — you can spot it from the opposite shore. The 30-meter round tower, built during the Viking raids, served as both bell tower and refuge. Wander among the ruins — the little St Kevin's Kitchen with its stone roof is the most photogenic. The site survived Vikings, Normans and the English who destroyed it in 1398.

Take the detour through Glendalough valley to the upper lake. This is where Saint Kevin lived as a hermit for seven years in a rocky cavity perched 10 meters above the water. Legend tells he prayed so long, motionless in the icy waters, that birds came to nest in his outstretched hands — the poet Seamus Heaney, Nobel Prize winner, wrote a famous poem about it. The setting is magical, especially early morning when mist drifts between the two lakes.

activities.text: If you have time, walk a section of the Wicklow Way, Ireland's oldest long-distance trail. The route around Glendalough's two lakes suits everyone — allow about 90 minutes for the loop. For the more energetic, the trail climbing towards Poulanass Waterfall offers a rewarding payoff en route.

Driving from Dublin, take the scenic Sally Gap road that crosses the Wicklow Mountains. This deserted route snakes between ochre peatlands and deep valleys — you'd think you were on the moon. Stop at the Lough Tay viewpoint, nicknamed the 'Guinness lake' because of its black waters bordered by white sand. It's one of Wicklow's most photographed panoramas.

visits.text: Kilkenny Castle has dominated the River Nore since 1260. Built by the Normans, it served as the Butler family seat for over 600 years. In 1967, the last Marquess of Ormonde sold it to the town for the symbolic sum of £50. Now restored to its 1830s appearance, you'll tour the portrait gallery, library and Victorian bedrooms. Fun fact: Anne Boleyn's grandmother, Lady Margaret Butler, was born here. The 20-hectare park is freely accessible — perfect for a picnic.

Twenty minutes south, Jerpoint Abbey is one of Ireland's finest Cistercian monasteries, founded in 1180. The cloister preserves remarkable Romanesque sculptures — look for knights and dragons on the pillars. The Norman lords' tombs are impressive. The neighbouring village of Thomastown also merits a look for its pretty streets lined with colourful houses.

Walk up Kilkenny's Medieval Mile, a kilometre of medieval streets between the castle and St Canice's Cathedral. This 13th-century cathedral has a round tower you can climb — the view over the city from the top is the best around. En route, you'll pass Rothe House, a perfectly preserved 1594 Tudor merchant's house with its recreated garden.

activities.text: Kilkenny has been brewing beer since the 14th century. Visit the Smithwick's Experience that traces this brewing tradition in the former St Francis Abbey. You'll learn that the Smithwick family started brewing here in 1710 on the site of an ancient Franciscan monastery. The final tasting lets you compare different ales — the Blonde is the most surprising.

Late afternoon, stroll through the castle park along the Nore. Kilkenny locals come here to jog and walk their dogs at sunset. If you've still got energy, head to the Kilkenny Design Centre in the castle's former stables — it's one of Ireland's best spots for contemporary craft.

visits.text: Start at the English Market, open since 1788 — it's one of the world's oldest municipal covered markets. The ornamental Princes Street entrance dates from 1862. You'll find spiced beef (Cork's speciality), drisheen (local blood pudding) and farmhouse cheeses. Head upstairs to the Farmgate Café to watch the action from the balcony — it's the market's best table. A fire destroyed the building in 1980, but it was rebuilt identically.

Saint Fin Barre's Cathedral, completed in 1879, is a neo-Gothic masterpiece by architect William Burges. Its three spires dominate the city's south side. The interior dazzles with the richness of its mosaics, stained glass and sculptures — Burges designed everything, down to the door handles. The site has been sacred since the 7th century, when Saint Fin Barre founded a monastery here.

Twenty minutes northwest of Cork, Blarney Castle is a medieval tower from 1446 built by Cormac MacCarthy, King of Munster. The visit's highlight: kissing the Blarney Stone, wedged high in the battlements. You have to lean backwards over the void — safety bars hold you, but it's still impressive. Legend has it that Queen Elizabeth I, exasperated by Lord Blarney's verbal evasions, invented the word 'blarney' to mean flattery. The gardens around the castle also deserve exploring, especially the Poison Garden with its toxic plants.

Head up to Shandon quarter on Cork's north hill. St Anne's Church and its octagonal 18th-century tower are the city's symbol. Climb the 132 steps and you can ring the famous Shandon Bells — locals say a true Corkonian must be born within earshot of their sound. The 360° view over Cork and the River Lee is the city's best.

Stop by the Crawford Art Gallery in the city centre. Entry's free and the Irish paintings collection is worth seeing — works by Jack B. Yeats, the poet's brother, through to contemporary artists. The building itself, a former 18th-century customs house, has character. The attached café serves carefully prepared local cuisine — Corkonians consider it one of the city's best canteens.

activities.text: Late afternoon, walk along the River Lee which divides into two arms around the city centre — Cork is actually built on an island. The south quay offers lovely perspectives on the colourful facades. If you have time, operators offer short cruises on the Lee to see the city from the water.

visits.text: Leaving Cork, you'll join the Wild Atlantic Way, that 2,500km coastal route hugging the Atlantic. The stretch between Cork and Kenmare gives you a taste of what's ahead — carved bays, emerald pastures and sheep indifferent to your passing. Stop at Moll's Gap for views over the MacGillycuddy's Reeks, Ireland's highest mountain range.

Kenmare — 'Neidín' in Irish, the 'little nest' — is one of Kerry's most charming villages. Laid out in 1775 by the Marquess of Lansdowne, the X-shaped plan with two main streets and triangular square is a rare example of a planned town in Ireland. The brightly painted facades and gourmet restaurants make it a perfect base for exploring the Ring of Kerry. Wander Henry Street and William Street to scout your evening spots.

Five minutes' walk from the centre, don't miss Kenmare stone circle — 15 standing stones dating from the Bronze Age, over 3,000 years ago. It's one of the largest megalithic circles in southwest Ireland. Its exact purpose remains mysterious — burial site or solar cult place, nobody knows. Access is free and the site is rarely crowded.

activities.text: If you arrive early enough, treat yourself to a scenic drive along part of the Ring of Kerry. This 179km loop around the Iveragh Peninsula is one of Ireland's most famous routes. Drive anti-clockwise to avoid the tour buses travelling the other direction. Stop at Ladies View — this viewpoint owes its name to Queen Victoria's ladies-in-waiting, amazed by the vista in 1861.

Late afternoon, operators offer rides in traditional jaunting cars (horse-drawn carriages) through Killarney National Park. These horse carriages have been a local institution since the 19th century. The driver will tell you stories — true or invented — throughout the ride between the lakes and rhododendrons. Allow about an hour for the classic circuit.

visits.text: The Slea Head Drive is a 47km loop that circles the western tip of the Dingle Peninsula — it's Ireland's westernmost point. Drive clockwise to avoid the buses. The cliffs plunge into the Atlantic facing the Blasket Islands, uninhabited since 1953. Stop at Dunmore Head for the most spectacular view, then at Coumeenoole Beach where scenes from the film 'Ryan's Daughter' were shot. Allow at least half a day.

Dingle is a colourful fishing port where Irish is still spoken daily. Signs are bilingual and traditional music echoes from the pubs by early evening. The compact harbour is walkable in an hour — painted facades, craft shops, seafood restaurants. It's one of the rare places in Ireland where you feel Gaelic is a living language, not a relic.

Gallarus Oratory, 10km from Dingle, is a small dry-stone chapel probably dating from the 11th or 12th century. Its corbelled construction — stones slot together without mortar and have stayed watertight for centuries — is a technical feat. The interior measures just 4.8m long by 3m wide, lit by a single window. Local legend says whoever passes through that window is guaranteed heaven. Site access is free if you avoid the paid visitor centre.

En route, stop at Inch Beach, a massive 5km sand spit jutting into Dingle Bay. It's one of Kerry's most beautiful beaches — surfers come for the waves and walkers for the space. Even in grey weather, the light is exceptional.

activities.text: From Dingle harbour, operators offer boat trips to watch dolphins in the bay. The Dingle Peninsula is one of Ireland's best spots for cetaceans — bottlenose dolphins, common dolphins and sometimes basking sharks. Trips last about 2 hours. Bring a windbreaker, even in summer.

In the evening, step into a local distillery producing artisan gin from wild plants gathered on the peninsula. The guided tour explains the distillation process and ends with a tasting. It's a good way to finish the day before dinner at one of the harbour's seafood restaurants.

visits.text: Muckross House is an 1843 Victorian mansion set beside Muckross Lake in Killarney National Park. Queen Victoria stayed three nights in 1861 — she slept on the ground floor for fear of fires. Period furniture remains in place and the guided tour traces daily life in a grand Irish estate. The Italianate gardens and rhododendrons that explode with colour in May-June are magnificent. Right next door, the traditional farm recreates Irish rural life from the 1920s to 1950s.

Five hundred metres from Muckross House, the ruins of Muckross Abbey date from 1448. The cloister is invaded by a centuries-old yew tree growing right in the centre — the tree has become the place's symbol. Gaelic chieftains and poets are buried here. The site is freely accessible and rarely crowded in the morning.

The Gap of Dunloe is a narrow 11km glacial valley between the MacGillycuddy's Reeks and Purple Mountain. The road snakes between dark lakes and moss-covered boulders — it's one of Kerry's wildest landscapes. You can traverse it by car, on foot or by jaunting car. The gap is so narrow in places that two cars struggle to pass. Arrive early morning to avoid the traffic.

activities.text: Operators offer boat tours on Killarney's three lakes — Lough Leane, Muckross Lake and Upper Lake. The crossing takes about an hour and gives you a unique perspective on the mountains and ancient forests bordering the shores. Some circuits combine the boat with a walk through the Gap of Dunloe — an excellent full day.

Don't leave Killarney without climbing to Torc Waterfall, 7km from the town centre on the N71 road. The 20-meter fall is nestled in a forest of centuries-old oaks. The trail from the car park takes just 5 minutes, but if you continue climbing the stone steps to the summit, the view over Killarney's lakes from up there amply rewards the effort.

visits.text: The Cliffs of Moher rise 214 meters above the Atlantic along an 8km stretch — it's Ireland's most visited natural site. On clear days, you'll see the Aran Islands and Galway Bay. The visitor centre, embedded in the hillside, presents an exhibition on the geology and wildlife. Tip: arrive before 10am or after 4pm to avoid the crowds. The coastal path heading south towards Hag's Head is less frequented and offers equally spectacular views.

The Burren is a lunar landscape of cracked limestone covering 250 square kilometres of County Clare. These grey rock slabs, called 'clints' and 'grikes', harbour unique flora — arctic orchids grow alongside Mediterranean plants, a botanical paradox that fascinates scientists. Walk on the pavements and observe the wildflowers bursting from the fissures. In spring, the display is particularly colourful.

Aillwee Caves, carved into the Burren's limestone, extend over a kilometre underground. You'll see stalactites, an underground waterfall and the remains of a hibernating brown bear — bones several thousand years old were found here. The guided tour lasts 35 minutes and the underground temperature stays constant at 10°C — bring a jumper.

Poulnabrone Dolmen stands in the middle of the Burren like a stone portal 5,800 years old. This megalithic tomb, built between 3800 and 3200 BC, contained the remains of 33 people. The horizontal slab balanced on two upright stones weighs several tonnes. It's one of Ireland's most photographed prehistoric monuments — and freely accessible.

activities.text: Doolin is the self-proclaimed capital of Irish traditional music. In the evening, the village's three historic pubs host live music sessions almost nightly — fiddle, concertina, bodhrán. The atmosphere is authentic, far from tourist folklore. Arrive early to get a seat as the rooms are small.

For walkers, the coastal path between Doolin and the Cliffs of Moher runs along the cliff edge for about 6km. It's one of Ireland's most beautiful coastal hikes — vertigo is guaranteed, but the trail is well marked. Allow 2 to 3 hours return.

visits.text: Galway is the west of Ireland's liveliest city — the Irish call it the 'cultural capital'. Start in the Latin Quarter around Quay Street and Shop Street, lined with colourful pubs, buskers (street musicians) and shops. The bohemian atmosphere recalls Paris's Latin Quarter, only more laid-back. The pedestrian streets bustle day and night.

The Spanish Arch is one of the last remnants of Galway's medieval walls, dating from 1584. It owes its name to maritime trade with Spain — Spanish ships came to load salted fish here. The surrounding quays are Galwegians' favourite spot for catching sun when it deigns to appear. Right next door, Galway City Museum traces the city's history from prehistory — entry is free.

Cross the bridge over the Corrib to see Galway Cathedral, completed in 1965 — it's one of the last great stone religious buildings constructed in Europe. Its copper dome and interior mosaics merit a 15-minute detour.

activities.text: Galway lives to the rhythm of music — practically every city-centre pub offers live Celtic music sessions, especially weekends. Push open the doors of pubs on Shop Street or Dominick Street and let yourself be carried along. The atmosphere differs from Dublin — more intimate, more spontaneous.

Saturday mornings, Galway Market sets up around St Nicholas Church. You'll find local oysters, farmhouse cheeses, artisan bread and Breton crêpes (yes, a Frenchman has settled here). It's the best spot for brunch before hitting the road again. On weekdays, the market is smaller but still pleasant.

visits.text: Kylemore Abbey is an 1868 neo-Gothic castle set beside a lake in the heart of Connemara — the image is so perfect it seems unreal. Mitchell Henry, a wealthy London doctor, built it for his wife Margaret. After her death in Egypt, he had a miniature cathedral built in her memory in the grounds. Since 1920, Benedictine nuns have occupied the place. Also visit the walled Victorian garden, restored identically — it's a 15-minute walk from the abbey.

Connemara National Park in Letterfrack covers 2,000 hectares of bogs, heathland and mountains. The visitor centre explains the geology and ecology of this wild region. It's the starting point for the Diamond Hill hike — the climb rewards the effort with coastal views (see activities).

Clifden, the 'capital of Connemara', is a lively small town nestled between the Twelve Bens and the Atlantic. The twin spires of its churches can be spotted from afar. Wander Main Street for its traditional pubs and wool shops. At the town's edge, a monument commemorates the first successful non-stop transatlantic flight by Alcock and Brown in 1919 — they landed in a bog right next to it.

activities.text: The Sky Road is a 12km scenic loop from Clifden that takes you to cliff tops with breathtaking views over the Atlantic and islands. The road climbs in hairpins above the coast — at every bend, the panorama changes. It's one of Ireland's most beautiful coastal roads and often deserted. Allow 30 to 45 minutes by car, more if you stop (and you will stop).

The Diamond Hill hike in Connemara National Park is the region's most popular. The marked trail climbs 3km to the summit at 442 meters. From the top, the view embraces the jagged coast, the islands of Inishbofin and the Connemara bogs. Allow 2h30 return. The trail is well constructed but the last 500 meters are rocky — good shoes are essential.

visits.text: Croagh Patrick is Ireland's sacred mountain — 764 meters above Clew Bay. According to tradition, Saint Patrick fasted there for 40 days in 441 and drove out the snakes. Every last Sunday of July, thousands of pilgrims climb to the summit, some barefoot. You don't have to go all the way to the top — the first third of the trail already offers magnificent views over Clew Bay's 365 islands. The start is from Murrisk car park.

Westport House is a 1730 Georgian mansion built on the foundations of a castle belonging to the family of 'pirate queen' Grace O'Malley. The interior preserves period furniture, family portraits and an impressive stucco-decorated hall. The gardens and surrounding park are pleasant for a stroll.

Westport town centre, designed by architect James Wyatt in the 18th century, is one of the rare examples of a planned town in Ireland. The Octagon (central square) and the tree-lined Mall along the Carrowbeg River give particular charm to this small town. The pubs on Bridge Street are renowned for their live music sessions.

activities.text: The Great Western Greenway is a 42km cycling trail between Westport and Achill Island, built on a former railway line. You don't have to do it all — the Westport-Newport section (11km) is flat, easy and hugs the Clew Bay coast. Bike rental shops offer their services in Westport. It's one of Ireland's most beautiful greenways.

If you prefer something calmer, farms around Westport offer visits where you'll discover sheep farming, shearing and sheepdog work. It's a very popular activity for families and a lovely way to understand rural life in the west of Ireland.

visits.text: Carrowmore megalithic cemetery is Ireland's largest — over 30 passage tombs and stone circles dating from 5,500 years ago, some older than Egypt's pyramids. The visitor centre explains Neolithic burial rituals. The central tomb, Listoghil, is the most impressive with its burial chamber covered by a cairn. The site sits in open countryside, with Knocknarea as a backdrop — they say the mythical Queen Maeve is buried at the summit.

Sligo Abbey, founded in 1253 by the Dominicans, preserves fine Gothic remains — notably an intact cloister and Renaissance sculptures on the altar. It's one of the best-preserved medieval buildings in northwest Ireland.

Benbulben is Sligo's emblematic mountain — its table-top silhouette, resembling an anvil or a ship's prow, can be spotted from miles away. This limestone formation sculpted by glaciers reaches 526 meters. This is where poet W.B. Yeats asked to be buried — his grave is in Drumcliff churchyard, at the mountain's foot. The epitaph he chose himself says: 'Cast a cold eye on life, on death. Horseman, pass by.'

The museum dedicated to Yeats, housed in the Yeats Memorial Building in the town centre, traces the life and work of Ireland's greatest poet, Nobel Prize winner for Literature in 1923. Even if you're unfamiliar with his poetry, the exhibition captures well the mystical atmosphere that nourished his work — Sligo was his land of inspiration.

activities.text: Strandhill Beach, 8km from Sligo, is the region's surf spot — Atlantic waves are powerful and consistent. Even if you don't surf, the walk on this wind-swept beach is invigorating. Operators offer lessons for beginners. After your session, head to the village's seaweed baths — a hot bath in water filled with algae, a centuries-old local tradition.

Follow the Yeats Trail, a marked literary route linking the places that inspired the poet — Lough Gill lake, Innisfree island (which he made famous in a poem), Lissadell manor. It's a beautiful way to discover the Sligo landscape through an artist's eyes. You can do part of it by car and the most striking sections on foot.

visits.text: Donegal Castle, in the town centre, was built in the 15th century by the O'Donnell clan, then remodelled by an English settler in the 17th century — the result is a unique blend of Gaelic tower and Jacobean manor. The guided tour recounts the turbulent history of the O'Donnells, who burned their own castle in 1607 rather than leave it to the English. The Diamond, the triangular square in front of the castle, is the small town's lively heart.

The Slieve League cliffs, 50km west of Donegal, rise to 601 meters — nearly three times the height of the Cliffs of Moher, making them Europe's highest sea cliffs. Yet they're far less frequented. The main viewpoint is accessible by car, but if you're a confident walker, the One Man's Path trail along the ridge is extraordinary — narrow, vertiginous and spectacular. In clear weather, you can see all the way to Connemara.

Warning: Slieve League (west) and Glenveagh (north) lie in opposite directions from Donegal Town — allow 1h30 driving between the two. If you want to do both, leave very early morning for Slieve League, then continue to Glenveagh in the afternoon. Otherwise, choose one or the other. Glenveagh National Park protects 16,000 hectares of wild glacial valley. The 1870 Victorian castle, set beside a lake ringed by mountains, is accessible by shuttle from the visitor centre. The gardens, created in the 1960s, harbour exotic plants that shouldn't survive at this latitude — the valley's microclimate protects them.

activities.text: Donegal's coast is part of the Wild Atlantic Way and offers some of Ireland's wildest coastal walking trails. Even a short 30-minute walk along the cliffs around Slieve League will give you the feeling of being at the world's edge. The wind is often fierce — dress accordingly.

Donegal is renowned for its tweed, hand-woven for generations. Visit one of the region's weaving workshops to see craftspeople working on traditional looms. The fabric colours — greens, browns, blues — are inspired directly by the surrounding landscape. It's a good place to take home an authentic souvenir.

visits.text: On the route between Donegal and Belfast (218km, about 3 hours), stop at the Ulster American Folk Park, an open-air museum in Omagh, right on your route. Over 30 reconstructed buildings trace three centuries of Irish emigration to America — from Ulster thatched cottages to Pennsylvania log cabins. You'll even board a full-scale replica of an emigrant ship to feel the cramped conditions of the crossing. The museum is built around the birthplace of Thomas Mellon, founder of the Mellon banking dynasty of Pittsburgh. Costumed guides bake bread on griddles and recount daily life. Allow 2 hours for the visit.

Titanic Belfast is the city's unmissable museum — a spectacular building shaped like a ship's prow, built on the very site of the Harland & Wolff shipyards where the Titanic was assembled. The exhibition across nine galleries traces the liner's construction, voyage and sinking. The original dry dock, visible outside, is impressive — this is where the ship was launched in 1911. Book your tickets online. Allow at least 2 hours.

Belfast's political murals are both street art and scars of history. The 'Peace Walls' — these walls that still today separate Catholic and Protestant neighbourhoods — reach up to 8 meters high. The murals in Falls Road (republican side) and Shankill Road (loyalist side) each tell their version of the Troubles. Operators offer black taxi tours with former residents from both communities as guides — it's the best way to understand this recent and complex history.

The Ulster Museum, at the entrance to the Botanic Gardens, is Northern Ireland's main museum. Entry is free. Collections cover art, archaeology and natural sciences. Among the most remarkable pieces: treasures recovered from the Spanish Armada wrecked off the Donegal coast in 1588, and an Egyptian mummy. Allow 1 to 2 hours depending on your interests.

activities.text: St George's Market, open Friday, Saturday and Sunday, is Belfast's last Victorian covered market. The 1896 building is worth seeing in itself. Saturday mornings, stalls overflow with local produce, street food and crafts. Live musicians play between the aisles — the atmosphere is joyous and Belfastians come with their families.

The Cathedral Quarter is Belfast's trendy district — art galleries, cocktail bars, street art and fashionable restaurants. In the evening, it's the city's liveliest spot. Wander Hill Street and Commercial Court for the atmosphere. If you're looking for a traditional pub, the Duke of York in a cobbled alley is an institution.

visits.text: Kilmainham Gaol is Dublin's most moving visit. This 1796 prison held the great names of Ireland's independence struggle. In May 1916, fourteen leaders of the Easter Rising were shot in the stonebreakers' yard. James Connolly, too wounded to stand, was strapped to a chair for his execution. Joseph Plunkett married his fiancée Grace in the prison chapel the night before his execution. The guided tour lasts an hour and the queue can be long — book online at least 28 days in advance.

Dublin Castle, in the city centre, was the seat of English power in Ireland for over 700 years, from its construction in 1204 until independence in 1922. The State Apartments — the ceremonial rooms — are sumptuously decorated. The neo-Gothic Royal Chapel and the 13th-century Record Tower are the oldest parts of the complex. Today, the castle hosts Irish presidential ceremonies.

End your stay at the Jameson Distillery on Bow Street in the Smithfield quarter. Irish whiskey differs from its Scottish cousin through triple distillation that makes it smoother. The guided tour explains the process and ends with a comparative tasting — you'll learn to distinguish a Jameson from a bourbon and a scotch. The brick building is the original production site, founded in 1780.

activities.text: If you have time in the morning, Glasnevin Cemetery is worth the detour. Founded in 1832, it's Ireland's largest cemetery — 1.5 million people are buried here, including Daniel O'Connell, Michael Collins and Éamon de Valera. The 51-meter round tower built in O'Connell's honour dominates the site. The adjacent museum offers fascinating guided tours that trace Ireland's history through its dead.

For your last hours in Dublin, walk down Grafton Street, the main pedestrian shopping street. Buskers — street musicians — are a tradition here. Ed Sheeran made his debut here. Continue to St Stephen's Green park for a final peaceful stroll in the heart of Dublin before completing your Irish circuit.

seo.h1_title: Ireland Road Trip: Capitals, Coasts and Mountains
seo.intro_paragraph: Wander through Dublin between Georgian facades and thousand-year-old manuscripts, then plunge into the misty valleys of Glendalough around its 6th-century monastic ruins. Lose yourself in Kilkenny's medieval lanes, breathe Cork's creative energy and let the Dingle Peninsula blend sheer mountains with untamed ocean. From wild Connemara to the Cliffs of Moher, from Galway's pubs to Belfast's murals, this 15-day road trip circles the entire Emerald Isle.
seo.seo_hook: Twelve-century illuminated manuscripts, vertiginous cliffs facing the Atlantic, music sessions in centuries-old pubs and breathtaking coastal roads — Ireland in 15 days, south to north.
featured_list: Dublin: the Book of Kells manuscript dating from the year 800
Cliffs of Moher: 214 meters of cliffs facing the Atlantic
Ring of Kerry: 179km of panoramic coastal road
Belfast: Titanic museum on the very site of the original shipyards

highlights: Book of Kells at Trinity College: illuminated manuscript from the year 800 in a 65-meter library
Cliffs of Moher: 214 meters above the Atlantic along an 8km stretch
Slea Head Drive: 47km of coastal road at Europe's western edge
Titanic Belfast: world-class museum on the original shipyard site

itineraries.title: Secret Ireland Road Trip: Hidden Villages & Wild Coasts
itineraries.subtitle: From Dalkey to Dublin, 10 days through Ireland's hidden villages—fishing ports, forgotten abbeys, secret cliffs and the Burren's moonscapes.
itineraries.notes: Feasible year-round but some activities (boats to Dalkey Island, Dunguaire Castle banquets, Caherconnell sheepdog demonstrations) are seasonal (April-October). Narrow roads in Wicklow and on Achill Island—careful driving recommended.

visits.text: Dalkey was once Dublin's main port—in medieval times, the Liffey was too silted for large ships, so merchants built seven fortified castles to store their goods. Only two remain standing on Castle Street. Wander the lanes of this coastal village dubbed Ireland's 'Amalfi Coast'—you'll pass bookshops, delis and pubs where local celebrities are regulars. If you're here on a Tuesday, it's market day, a tradition running since 1482.

Step inside Dalkey Castle, the only one of the seven medieval fortifications still standing and open to visitors. Built in the 14th century, it was nicknamed 'Goat Castle' by the Cheever family in the 1600s. The guided tour is theatrical—costumed actors demonstrate the 'Murder Hole' (a defensive trapdrop), archery techniques, and Tudor barber-surgeon remedies. Climb the battlements for sweeping views over Dublin Bay. Book online—places fill fast in summer.

From Coliemore Harbour, catch a boat to Dalkey Island—25 hectares of uninhabited land 300 metres offshore. The island has been occupied for over 6,000 years, predating Stonehenge. You'll see the ruins of St. Begnet's Church (7th century), a Napoleonic Martello tower, and wild goats who rule the rocks. Vikings ran a slave trading post here in the 10th century—coins from King Edgar's reign (959-975) were found during Victorian excavations. Allow 75 minutes return including the crossing.

Don't miss Sorrento Park, a discreet promontory tucked between Dalkey and Killiney—the entrance on Coliemore Road is easy to overlook. From its benches, you'll gaze down at Dalkey Island and across to the Wicklow Mountains. It's the kind of view that local residents contemplate each morning from their villas hidden behind the palm trees.

activities.text: Walk up Killiney Hill—20 minutes from the car park brings you to the summit. The hill rises to 140 metres and offers 360° views: Dublin to the north, the Wicklow Mountains to the south, and on clear days, Snowdon in Wales to the east. At the top you'll find a 1742 obelisk, built as a famine relief project, and the 'Pyramid of Dublin' (1852)—legend says if you walk around it uphill, then make a wish facing Dalkey Island, it'll come true. Arrive early on weekends when the car park fills quickly.

Head down for a swim at the Forty Foot, the historic bathing spot in Sandycove. This natural rock pool, once men-only, now welcomes everyone—year-round regulars plunge in even in winter. The water is bracing (10-15°C in summer), but the experience is worth the thermal shock. James Joyce set the opening of Ulysses here. Bring shoes that can handle the rocks.

visits.text: Enniskerry is one of Wicklow's most photographed villages—a handful of colourful houses clustered around a triangle of green, with St. Patrick's Church watching over the scene. The village serves as the gateway to the Powerscourt estate and the Wicklow Mountains. Walk around it in twenty minutes, but take time to stop in one of the cafés for a homemade scone before tackling the day ahead.

Six kilometres from Enniskerry, Powerscourt Waterfall plunges 121 metres down granite rock—it's Ireland's highest waterfall. The valley was carved by a glacier, and the River Dargle cascades down in a fan year-round. In 1821, Viscount Powerscourt had the waterfall dammed to impress King George IV—the king never came, and when the water was released, it swept away the bridge. Visit after rain when the flow is at its peak. Warning: no phone signal at the car park, so if you're coming by taxi, arrange your return in advance.

The Great Sugar Loaf rises to 501 metres—its conical silhouette looks volcanic, but it's actually a Cambrian quartzite dome. The ascent is short (about 1 hour return) but steep, with a rocky scramble at the summit. The view sweeps from the coast at Bray to Greystones, across the Wicklow Mountains and Dublin Bay. On clear days, you'll spot Wales. Don't leave valuables in your car—thefts have been reported at the car park.

activities.text: If you're an experienced walker, tackle Djouce Mountain (725m). The trail starts from Crone Woods and climbs through forest before emerging onto heather moorland with plunging views down to Powerscourt Waterfall below. Allow 3-4 hours for the full loop. The terrain can be muddy and exposed to wind—bring hiking boots and a windproof layer.

Detour to the National Botanic Gardens at Kilmacurragh, east of Wicklow. This 21-hectare estate, founded in 1712 by the Acton family, houses 180 rhododendron species and over 420 varieties—it's the national collection. The acidic soil and mild microclimate allow Himalayan and southern hemisphere species to thrive where they'd fail elsewhere in Ireland. You'll also see giant sequoias and a dawn redwood once thought extinct. The ruins of the Queen Anne house (1697) add a romantic touch. Free entry, guided tours at noon and 3pm from April to October.

visits.text: The Rock of Cashel is visible for miles—this 109-metre limestone outcrop dominates the golden plain of Tipperary with the silhouette of a phantom cathedral. It's here that St. Patrick supposedly converted King Aengus in the 5th century, accidentally piercing the king's foot with his crosier—the king said nothing, believing it was part of the ritual. Brian Boru was crowned High King here in 978. Arrive early morning or late afternoon to avoid the tour buses—after 10am it gets crowded. Tickets for Cormac's Chapel (guided tour only) are sold at the entrance and sell out quickly.

Cormac's Chapel, consecrated in 1134, is the Rock's jewel—it's the only Romanesque building in Ireland to preserve its original frescoes, nearly 900 years old. Two carpenters sent by the Abbot of Regensburg contributed to its construction, hence the Germanic influences. Beside it, the 28-metre round tower (circa 1100) is the oldest monument on the site—its entrance is 4 metres above ground because its foundations only go down one metre. The Gothic cathedral (1235-1270) lost its roof in 1749, when Archbishop Price had it removed—a decision that still rankles.

Five hundred metres below the Rock, cross the field (sometimes muddy, sometimes with cows) to reach Hore Abbey—the last Cistercian abbey founded in Ireland before the Reformation, in 1272. Legend says the archbishop banished the Benedictines after dreaming they wanted to behead him. In reality, he replaced them with Cistercians from Mellifont and gifted them land, mills, and two jugs of beer from each brew of Cashel's 38 breweries. The abbey is freely accessible—you'll often have it to yourself.

Push on to Fethard, 16km east of Cashel—this Norman village has Ireland's best-preserved medieval walls, with over 90% of the original ramparts still standing. The walls date from 1292 and rise to 7.8 metres in places. Follow the Heritage Trail along the River Clashawley—you'll pass Holy Trinity Church (13th century, still active), the Augustinian abbey and three Sheela-na-gigs, those mysterious medieval carvings whose exact meaning nobody knows. In 1650, Cromwell marched on Fethard, but the town surrendered—which is what saved its walls.

activities.text: If you have time, detour through the Glen of Aherlow, a valley nestled between the Galtee Mountains and the Slievenamuck hills. Waymarked trails wind through the forests—the Christ the King loop offers open views across the entire valley. You'll encounter only sheep and blackbirds.

Before leaving Cashel, walk around the town—the lanes off Main Street hide 15th-century merchant houses and remnants of the town walls (built between 1319 and 1324). The BrúBorú Cultural Centre, at the foot of the Rock, hosts traditional music evenings in season.

visits.text: Kinvara is a small fishing port on the south shore of Galway Bay—the kind of village where colourful houses reflect in the harbour water at high tide. The town comes alive in summer during Cruinniú na mBád, a gathering of traditional Galway hooker boats. Stroll along the quay and through the lanes—you'll find artisan galleries and pocket-sized pubs.

Dunguaire Castle sits on a rocky spur at the water's edge, 300 metres from the village. Built in 1520 by the O'Hynes clan, this 23-metre tower takes its name from King Guaire, 7th-century ruler of Connacht, famed for his generosity—legend says dishes flew from his table to feed the poor. In the 20th century, poet Oliver St John Gogarty bought it and made it a meeting place for Yeats, Shaw and Synge. Climb to the roof for views over Kinvara and the bay—mind the narrow passages. Medieval banquets are held in the evenings from April to October—book ahead.

Head into the Burren—twenty minutes from Kinvara, you enter a lunar landscape of fissured limestone where Arctic and Mediterranean orchids grow side by side. Stop at Poulnabrone Dolmen—this 5,800-year-old portal tomb stands alone amid the stone slabs. Excavations revealed the bones of 33 people and a newborn. The site is freely accessible, but arrive before 10am or after 4pm to avoid the crowds.

activities.text: Walk the Flaggy Shore between Kinvara and New Quay—Nobel laureate Seamus Heaney wrote a poem about it. The walk follows limestone slabs that slope into the sea. You'll see swans, herons and cormorants. Perfect late in the day when the only sound is the wind.

Explore the quieter eastern Burren. The turloughs (seasonal lakes) and clints-and-grikes (fissured pavements) shelter rare flora—from May to July, wild orchids, gentians and thyme bloom between the stones. Local naturalists offer guided walks.

map_keywords: Green Road Burren Way Black Head

visits.text: Ballyvaughan is a pocket-sized village perched on the edge of Galway Bay, at the northern fringe of the Burren. A handful of houses, a quay, two pubs and an end-of-the-world atmosphere. It's your base for exploring the Burren—everything's within 20 minutes. Stop at the little harbour to watch the fishing boats come in with the day's lobster catch.

Fifteen minutes south, Caherconnell Stone Fort is one of the Burren's best-preserved—a 1,000-year-old stone ring, inhabited until the 16th century. Excavations unearthed objects dating back to the Bronze Age. The Davoren family, who've farmed the neighbouring land for five generations, offer guided tours of the fort and sheepdog demonstrations—border collies herd sheep to whistle commands in the Burren landscape. Book ahead, it's one of Clare's most popular activities.

Head to Doolin Cave, 25 minutes from Ballyvaughan. You'll descend 125 steps to reach, 20 metres underground, the Great Stalactite—7.3 metres long, roughly 10 tonnes, the longest free-hanging stalactite in Europe. Discovered in 1952 by two cavers, it remained hidden for millennia. The guided tour lasts 45 minutes and the temperature stays at 11°C year-round—bring a jumper. The pottery made on-site uses glacial clay containing pollen from prehistoric flowers.

activities.text: Walk the Green Road, an old track linking Ballyvaughan to Black Head along the Burren coast. This section of the Burren Way winds between dry-stone walls and limestone terraces—you'll overlook Galway Bay with the Aran Islands on the horizon. The path follows the ancient route locals took before the coastal road was built. Allow 2-3 hours one-way—turn back when you like.

If you're into botany, join a guided Burren walk—from May to July, the limestone pavements burst with orchids, gentians and pimpernels. The Burren holds 75% of Ireland's wildflower species on just 1% of its land area. The limestone absorbs daytime heat and releases it at night, creating near-Mediterranean conditions in the heart of the Atlantic.

visits.text: Cong sits on an isthmus between Lough Corrib and Lough Mask—a village of 900 souls threaded by underground rivers that bubble up here and there between the stones. The Augustinian abbey, founded in the 12th century by High King Turlough O'Connor, stands at the village heart. Ruaidrí Ua Conchobair, the last High King of Ireland, spent his final years here before dying at the end of the 12th century. Wander among the carved arches and cloisters—centuries of erosion have given the stone an almost organic texture.

From the abbey, a footbridge crosses the river to the Monk's Fishing House—a small 15th- or 16th-century stone hut built on stilts over the water. The monks fished through a trapdoor in the floor, and a rope linked to the monastery kitchen rang a bell when a fish bit. Continue to the grounds of Ashford Castle—built in 1228 by the Anglo-Norman de Burgos, transformed into a neo-Gothic castle by the Guinness family in the 19th century. The 140-hectare estate is open to the public (except during private events)—the Kinlough woods lead down to the shores of Lough Corrib.

Film fans will recognise Cong as the village where John Ford shot 'The Quiet Man' in 1951 with John Wayne and Maureen O'Hara. The Quiet Man Museum, in a cottage that recreates the film's sets, immerses you in 1920s rural Ireland. On the square, the statue of Wayne carrying O'Hara is the village's photo spot. Even if the film means nothing to you, the museum offers a fascinating glimpse of daily life in post-war Ireland.

A kilometre and a half west of the village, in a pine forest, the Pigeon Hole caves are reached by a waymarked trail from the abbey. Stone steps (steep and slippery) descend into a karst network where underground rivers link Lough Mask to Lough Corrib. No equipment needed, but proper footwear is essential.

activities.text: Follow the Cong Forest Nature Trail—a 3km loop through woods bordering the Ashford estate. The path runs alongside an unfinished canal, remnant of a Great Famine project (1845-1848) meant to link the two lakes but never worked because the water seeps through the porous limestone. The ancient trees, ferns and underground streams create an enchanted-forest atmosphere.

Lough Corrib, Ireland's second-largest lake, is renowned for fly fishing—brown trout and salmon. Local operators offer guided boat outings. Even if you don't fish, a walk along the shores at sunset is worthwhile.

visits.text: On the flanks of Slievemore (671m), the Deserted Village stretches in a line of 80 to 100 abandoned stone houses—mortarless walls, a single room per dwelling. The village was abandoned during the Great Famine of 1845, when families migrated to Dooagh by the sea. But young people continued to bring cattle up here each summer ('booley-ing') until the 1940s. Walk among the ruins and the 'lazy beds' (potato cultivation ridges) still visible—it's a silent place that tells the story of harsh rural Irish life better than any museum.

Keem Bay, at the end of a vertiginous switchback road, is a horseshoe beach wedged between the cliffs of Benmore and the Croaghaun massif. The sand is golden, the water crystalline (and cold). Basking sharks swim here each summer—you might spot one from the shore. In the 1950s, they were fished here for the oil in their livers, exported to the aerospace industry. Above the beach, archaeological digs revealed a mysterious 40-house village, inhabited until 1838 and completely forgotten. Arrive early in summer—the car park is chaos after 9am.

The Atlantic Drive loops around the southern part of Achill on a narrow, spectacular coastal road. You'll hug cliffs battered by waves, pass deserted beaches and isolated cottages with views over Clew Bay. Allow an hour without stops, but plan for double—you'll stop at every bend for photos.

activities.text: If you're an experienced hiker, climb to the Croaghaun cliffs—at 688 metres, they're Europe's third-highest sea cliffs, after Hornelen in Norway and Cap Enniberg in the Faroes. The trail starts from Keem Bay and climbs steeply for 2-3 hours. There's no marked path on the summit—it's a traverse across moorland and boulders. The wind can be fierce and the cliff edges are dangerous. Don't attempt this hike in bad weather or alone.

Achill is a good spot for seabird watching—gannets, guillemots and puffins nest on the west coast cliffs between April and July. From Keem Bay or Saddle Head, you can also spot grey seals on the rocks below. Bring binoculars.

visits.text: Mullaghmore is a hamlet clinging to a wild Sligo peninsula, facing Donegal Bay. The small stone harbour, built between 1822 and 1841 by Lord Palmerston (British Prime Minister), is lined by nearly 2km of sandy beach. From the quay, the view sweeps across to Benbulben, that flat-topped mountain formed during the Ice Age that dominates the Sligo landscape.

Classiebawn Castle stands on a grassy knoll a hundred metres from the sea—with its conical turret, it looks like something out of Disney. Built from 1860 in Donegal sandstone for Lord Palmerston, it was completed in 1874. The castle was Lord Mountbatten's summer residence until his assassination in 1979, when an IRA bomb blew up his fishing boat in the harbour. The castle is privately owned and closed to the public, but you'll see it close-up from the peninsula road—it's one of Ireland's most photographed castles, especially with Benbulben in the background.

Stop at Drumcliff churchyard, 20 minutes south—this is where W.B. Yeats, 1923 Nobel laureate in literature, is buried. His grave bears the epitaph he chose himself: 'Cast a cold eye on life, on death. Horseman, pass by!' The 6th-century church and the High Medieval Celtic cross complete the scene, with Benbulben rising just behind.

activities.text: The Mullaghmore Head loop is one of the northwest's finest coastal walks—a 4.5km circuit on the cliffs facing the Atlantic. In big swells (October to March), waves can exceed 15 metres here—in March 2012, surfers from around the world rode 15-metre waves. Even without surf, the spectacle of the thrashing ocean from the path is gripping.

If weather permits, explore the flanks of Benbulben—this 526-metre table mountain is said in mythology to shelter the Fianna warriors and the tomb of the lovers Diarmuid and Gráinne. The summit is a wind-battered limestone plateau. Approach trails start from the north side. Warning: the faces are sheer and the terrain can be dangerous in wet conditions—check locally before ascending.

visits.text: Carrick-on-Shannon is the capital of Leitrim—a small riverside town of 4,500 perched on the Shannon, the longest river in the British Isles (360km). The lively quay is the departure point for cruises and houseboats heading upriver. In the evenings, the pubs along Bridge Street and Main Street come alive with live music—it's a good place for a traditional session.

Take a cruise on the Shannon—boats offer one- to two-hour outings that wind upriver between reeds and floodplain meadows. You'll pass under stone bridges and alongside wooded islets where grey herons nest. It's the best way to understand why this river has shaped all of central Ireland's life for millennia.

Don't walk past Costello Chapel without stopping—squeezed between two buildings on Main Street, this tiny 5m by 3.5m chapel is Europe's smallest and the world's second-smallest. Edward Costello had it built in 1879 as a mausoleum for his wife Mary Josephine, who died aged 47. He'd had her body embalmed and kept it with the nuns while waiting for the building to be finished. The two coffins, covered in thick glass plates, are visible beneath the floor. It's Leitrim's most touching love story—the Irish 'Taj Mahal' in miniature.

activities.text: Operators offer houseboat rentals for half-day or longer trips on the Shannon and its connected lakes. No licence required—they explain the navigation basics before you set off. It's a unique way to see the Irish countryside from the water, at your own pace.

Thirty minutes north, Lough Key Forest Park offers forest trails, suspension bridges in the canopy and a tree canopy walk 9 metres above the ground. The 350-hectare park stretches around the ruins of Rockingham Castle, on the shores of Lough Key. It's also a good pike fishing spot if that tempts you.

visits.text: On your way back to Dublin, stop at Brú na Bóinne to visit Newgrange—this 5,200-year-old passage tomb is older than the pyramids of Giza and Stonehenge. The 85-metre-diameter cairn is faced with white quartz stones, and its 19-metre interior passage is aligned so that at the winter solstice, a beam of sunlight illuminates the burial chamber for exactly 17 minutes. Visits are guided tours only from the Visitor Centre—book online, slots sell out weeks ahead in summer.

Detour through Trim, 15 minutes from Newgrange—Trim Castle is Ireland's largest Anglo-Norman castle, built from 1176 by Hugh de Lacy. Its 20-metre keep dominates the River Boyne and several scenes from 'Braveheart' were filmed here in 1995. The town also preserves medieval walls, a 14th-century Yellow Steeple and the ruins of Newtown Trim abbey—all walkable in an hour.

For your last evening, skip Temple Bar and head to Smithfield—this neighbourhood north of the Liffey is the Dubliners' Dublin. The old horse market square (the last sale was held just a few years ago) is surrounded by converted distilleries, independent cafés and galleries. The Lighthouse Cinema screens arthouse films in a former industrial building. It's the kind of neighbourhood where you won't run into other tourists.

activities.text: The Iveagh Gardens, steps from St Stephen's Green, are Dublin's secret garden—most tourists don't even know they exist. Designed in 1865, these formal gardens hide a waterfall, a box maze, a rose garden and lawns where Dubliners come to read at lunchtime. Entry is free and discreet—look for the gate on Clonmel Street.

End your trip with an exploration of Dublin's vintage markets—the Dublin Flea Market and weekend neighbourhood markets offer vinyl, crafts, street food and improbable finds. It's the best way to bring home a souvenir that doesn't come from a tourist shop.

seo.h1_title: Secret Ireland Road Trip: Hidden Villages & Wild Coasts
seo.intro_paragraph: Explore Dalkey's aristocratic coastal charm, walk the wild reliefs of Enniskerry, contemplate the Rock of Cashel from the forgotten ruins of Hore Abbey. Join the colourful harbour of Kinvara and the lunar landscape of the Burren, traverse the cinematic village of Cong, trace the vertiginous cliffs of Achill Island, follow the wave-battered peninsula of Mullaghmore, cruise the Shannon at Carrick-on-Shannon, then loop back to Dublin via the Neolithic treasures of Newgrange.
seo.seo_hook: Stone cottages tucked in valleys, forgotten abbeys standing against the Atlantic, secret cliffs where only sheep watch you pass—the Ireland tourists never see.
featured_list: Dalkey: former Viking port with seven medieval castles on Castle Street
Kinvara: fishing harbour below Dunguaire Castle (1520)
Cong: The Quiet Man film village between two lakes and a royal abbey
Mullaghmore: wild peninsula with giant waves facing Benbulben mountain

highlights: Dalkey Castle: theatrical guided tour in one of Dublin's seven medieval port castles
Hore Abbey: Ireland's last Cistercian abbey, alone facing the ruins in a field
Croaghaun Cliffs: Europe's third-highest sea cliffs at 688 metres
Costello Chapel: Europe's smallest chapel, a love story frozen in stone since 1879
